title: Classic & Efficient CNN Architectures Capstone
concept: This capstone turns Units 112-123 into one practical computer-vision story: classic CNN families taught the main architecture ideas, efficient families taught budget-aware design, and transfer learning turned pretrained backbones into reusable tools for real tasks.

exercises.prompt: Recap: when does ImageNet-style transfer learning usually work most smoothly?

options.text: When the new task is visually similar to the natural-photo world the backbone was pretrained on
When the model is forbidden from using a task head
When the target data is not image data at all

exercises.explain: Exactly — and that practical reuse mindset is the final step in this whole architecture phase.

cards.title: These architectures are now a toolbox, not a museum tour
cards.body: At first, LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, MobileNet, EfficientNet, and transfer learning can feel like a list of names. Now they fit together. You can see how CNN design evolved and how those design choices affect the backbone you would actually pick for a real vision problem.

cards.title: Phase 4 in one sentence
cards.body: Phase 4 showed how CNN architecture moved from early proof-of-concept stacks to deeper and smarter feature hierarchies, then to efficiency-aware backbones, and finally to practical reuse through transfer learning, backbone selection, and fine-tuning.

cards.title: From car history to choosing the right vehicle
cards.body: Learning car history matters more once you can answer real questions like: city scooter or pickup truck? CNN history is similar. The value is not memorizing names. The value is knowing why one design is simple, another is deep, another is efficient, and another is a better transfer-learning backbone for your route.

cards.title: Units 112-123, in one practical arc
cards.body: Unit 112: LeNet showed the early conv-plus-pooling recipe. Unit 113: AlexNet proved deep CNNs at ImageNet scale. Unit 114: VGG made depth simple and repeatable. Unit 115: Inception used parallel branches for smarter compute. Unit 116: ResNet used residual paths to make deep training easier. Unit 117: ResNet variants turned residual backbones into a flexible family. Unit 118: DenseNet pushed feature reuse even harder. Unit 119: efficient CNNs treated compute budget as a first-class design goal. Unit 120: EfficientNet scaled depth, width, and resolution more systematically. Unit 121: NAS searched for strong architectures automatically. Unit 122: backbone selection matched architecture families to task and hardware constraints. Unit 123: transfer learning made those backbones reusable through pretraining, freezing, fine-tuning, and domain-aware adaptation.

cards.title: This phase was not trivia about famous model names
cards.body: A common myth is that architecture history is just benchmark lore. Really, it taught reusable decision ideas: how depth changes training, how skips preserve signal, how efficiency trades off with accuracy, how a backbone fits a downstream task, and when pretrained CNN features transfer well or poorly.

cards.title: What Phase 4 gave you
cards.body: You moved from naming architectures to reasoning about them.

left.text:
LeNet -> AlexNet -> VGG -> Inception
ResNet -> variants -> DenseNet
Efficient CNNs -> EfficientNet -> NAS

right.text:
Choose a backbone for the task
Reuse pretrained features when it helps
Freeze or fine-tune based on data, budget, and domain gap

exercises.prompt: Which summary best captures the story of Units 112-123?

options.text: CNNs evolved from early classic stacks into deeper, more efficient, and more reusable backbones, and transfer learning turned those backbones into practical tools for new vision tasks
The main lesson was that only one architecture should ever be used for every vision task
Architecture stopped mattering once ImageNet existed
Transfer learning replaced the need to think about backbones, compute, or domain gaps

exercises.explain: Exactly — the phase connected architecture design to real model selection and reuse.

exercises.prompt: True or false: After this phase, choosing a CNN backbone should depend on the task, hardware budget, and transfer-learning situation rather than only on the most famous model name.
exercises.explain: True — that practical judgment is the whole point of the phase-ending bridge.

exercises.sentence: A pretrained CNN usually contributes a reusable visual ___.
exercises.explain: Right — the backbone is the reusable feature extractor that later tasks can borrow.

exercises.prompt: Which statement gives the best bridge from this phase into training CNNs in practice?

options.text: Once you understand architecture families and transfer learning, the next practical step is learning how to actually train, fine-tune, regularize, and deploy those CNN choices well
Now that you know architecture names, training details no longer matter
The next phase should ignore data, optimization, and augmentation because backbone choice solved everything already

exercises.explain: Yes — architecture choice is only half of real practice; training decisions are the next half.

exercises.prompt: A learner says: 'I finished Units 112-123. What practical vision understanding do I have now?' Which answer is best?

options.text: You can explain how CNNs grew from LeNet and AlexNet into deeper families like VGG, Inception, ResNet, and DenseNet; why efficient designs such as MobileNet and EfficientNet matter; how NAS and backbone selection shape model choice; and how pretrained CNN backbones can be frozen or fine-tuned depending on data, budget, and domain gap
You mainly learned a list of model names, but not what any of them imply for real computer-vision work
You learned that transfer learning makes architecture choice irrelevant
You learned that every vision task should use the largest CNN and avoid pretraining

exercises.explain: Exactly. You can now connect classic CNN history to modern practical choices: what each family contributed, why efficiency changed architecture design, how to think in terms of reusable backbones, and when transfer learning should freeze, fine-tune, or be treated cautiously because of domain gap. Phase 4: Classic & Efficient CNN Architectures is complete. Next up: Training CNNs in Practice.
